description:
You are acting as a data-scientist who has inherited a messy set of “raw” CSV datasets scattered over several sub-directories.  
Your goal is to use the classic Unix pairing of “find … | xargs …” (plus, if you wish, “-exec”) to batch-clean the tree in four very small steps and then document the results.

Dataset layout  
/home/user/projects/data/raw contains exactly three year-folders that already exist when the session begins:

1. /home/user/projects/data/raw/2020  
   • q1_sales.csv – NON-empty CSV file  
   • q1_sales.csv.bak – redundant copy (must be deleted)  
   • empty.csv – **0-byte** file (must be deleted)  
   • README.txt – ignore

2. /home/user/projects/data/raw/2021  
   • q2_sales.csv – NON-empty CSV file  
   • q2_sales.csv.bak – redundant copy (must be deleted)  
   • empty.csv – **0-byte** file (must be deleted)  
   • .hidden – ignore

3. /home/user/projects/data/raw/2022  
   • q3_sales.csv – NON-empty CSV file  
   • q3_sales.csv.bak – redundant copy (must be deleted)  
   • empty.csv – **0-byte** file (must be deleted)

Required end-state  
A. Every “*.bak” file located anywhere beneath /home/user/projects/data/raw is permanently removed.  
B. Every **zero-byte** “*.csv” file located anywhere beneath the same tree is permanently removed.  
C. All other files remain untouched.  
D. A plain-text report called /home/user/projects/cleaned_files.log is generated that lists every remaining “*.csv” file, one absolute path per line, sorted lexicographically and terminated by a single newline. No extra blank lines or spaces.

The report MUST end up with precisely these three lines:

/home/user/projects/data/raw/2020/q1_sales.csv  
/home/user/projects/data/raw/2021/q2_sales.csv  
/home/user/projects/data/raw/2022/q3_sales.csv  

After your session ends, automated tests will verify BOTH:
1. The absence of all “.bak” and zero-byte “.csv” files.
2. The exact content (and only those three lines) of cleaned_files.log.

Restrictions / Hints  
• Achieve everything with at most four command invocations that make essential use of “find” and “xargs” (or “-exec”).  
• You do NOT have root; stay inside /home/user.  
• Do not hard-code lists of filenames; the checker will look for generalised “find” usage.  
• Whenever you create the log file, make sure its parent directory already exists; you do not need to create any new directories.

When you are done, you should be able to run:

cat /home/user/projects/cleaned_files.log  

…and see exactly the three lines shown above, nothing more, nothing less.
truth:

Files that must exist after successful completion:

/home/user/projects/cleaned_files.log (permissions 0644)
Exact content (including trailing newline, no extra lines):
/home/user/projects/data/raw/2020/q1_sales.csv
/home/user/projects/data/raw/2021/q2_sales.csv
/home/user/projects/data/raw/2022/q3_sales.csv

Files that must NOT exist after completion:
/home/user/projects/data/raw/2020/q1_sales.csv.bak
/home/user/projects/data/raw/2020/empty.csv
/home/user/projects/data/raw/2021/q2_sales.csv.bak
/home/user/projects/data/raw/2021/empty.csv

/home/user/projects/data/raw/2022/empty.csv

All other files remain untouched.
